questions.text: Who is the author of 'A Visit to Cambridge'?
questions.explanation: The chapter is an account written by the journalist and writer Firdaus Kanga.

questions.text: Which university is the setting for the meeting in the story?
questions.explanation: The author visited Cambridge, which he calls the 'metaphor for England'.

questions.text: Who did Firdaus Kanga meet during his visit?
questions.explanation: He met the world-famous astrophysicist Stephen Hawking.

questions.text: What physical disability did Firdaus Kanga have?
options: Brittle bones (osteogenesis imperfecta) and used a wheelchair
questions.explanation: Kanga, like Hawking, was a person with a physical disability who used a wheelchair.

questions.text: Stephen Hawking occupied the same chair once held by ________ at Cambridge.
questions.explanation: Hawking was the Lucasian Professor of Mathematics, a position Newton once held.

questions.text: How did Stephen Hawking communicate with the author?
options: Using a computer that spoke for him in a synthesized voice
questions.explanation: Hawking tapped buttons on a computer to select words which were then spoken by the machine.

questions.text: How much time did Hawking's assistant give Kanga for the interview?
questions.explanation: The assistant allowed thirty minutes, though the meeting lasted longer.

questions.text: What did Hawking say when Kanga asked if he found it brave to be who he was?
options: He said he had had no choice
questions.explanation: Hawking dismisses the idea of 'bravery' as a simple lack of alternative options.

questions.text: What was Hawking's response when asked if it annoyed him when people patronized him?
questions.explanation: Hawking admitted it was amusing, showing his sense of humor despite his condition.

questions.text: What did Kanga see in Hawking's face during the meeting?
options: Agony and a spirit that was very restless
questions.explanation: The author felt the intense intellectual energy trapped in a motionless body.

questions.text: What advice did Stephen Hawking give to disabled people?
options: They should try to be like everyone else
They should concentrate on what they are good at
They should participate in the Olympics
questions.explanation: He believed in focusing on one's strengths rather than struggling with impossible physical tasks.

questions.text: How did Kanga describe Hawking's body in the chapter?
options: Like a lantern whose walls are worn so thin you see only the light inside
questions.explanation: This beautiful metaphor highlights Hawking's brilliant mind over his fragile physical state.

questions.text: According to Hawking, what is a waste of time for the disabled?
questions.explanation: He felt that pushing disabled people into standard competitive physical activities was often futile.

questions.text: What did the author feel when he was leaving Hawking's house?
options: A sense of inspiration and relief
questions.explanation: Seeing someone as disabled as himself achieve so much gave Kanga great strength.

questions.text: Why did Kanga feel that Hawking was the most 'beautiful' man he had seen?
options: Because of his inner glow and brilliant spirit
questions.explanation: The beauty refers to the radiance of his intellect and soul.

questions.text: What happened when the thirty minutes of the interview were over?
options: Hawking offered him tea and showed him his garden
questions.explanation: Hawking was kind enough to extend the visit and spend more time with Kanga.

questions.text: What did Kanga do to thank Hawking at the end of the meeting?
options: He touched Hawking's shoulder and wheeled out
He promised to write a book
questions.explanation: It was a silent, respectful gesture of connection between two similar souls.

questions.text: The computer voice of Hawking had ________.
questions.explanation: The synthesized voice was functional but lacked the human quality of tone.

questions.text: Kanga's book from which this is taken is called:
questions.explanation: Firdaus Kanga's travelogue is titled 'Heaven on Wheels'.

questions.text: What did the author mean by 'the incandescence of a man'?
options: The inner light/brilliance of his personality
questions.explanation: It refers to the shining quality of Hawking's mind.

questions.text: How did Hawking feel when Kanga told him he was an inspiration to others?
options: It didn't make it any better for him
questions.explanation: Hawking pointed out that being an inspiration doesn't help the person suffering the pain.

questions.text: The meeting was a 'journey' for the author because:
options: It changed his perspective on life and disability
He moved from one room to another
He went to a new country
questions.explanation: It was a profound emotional and spiritual journey.

questions.text: Stephen Hawking is known for his work in:
questions.explanation: He was one of the greatest theoretical physicists/astrophysicists of our time.

questions.text: What was the name of the assistant who answered the phone?
questions.explanation: The text refers to him as 'a man' or 'assistant' but doesn't name him.

questions.text: The author felt ________ when Hawking's computer voice started.
questions.explanation: He felt a deep connection with the man who shared his struggles.

questions.text: Hawking's eyes were described as:
questions.explanation: Kanga felt Hawking's eyes were trying to communicate things that were difficult to put into words.

questions.text: What is the primary theme of the chapter?
options: The resilience of the human spirit over physical limitations
questions.explanation: The story focuses on how the mind can remain free despite a disabled body.

questions.text: The author says 'Cambridge was my metaphor for England'. This means:
options: To him, Cambridge represented the essence of England
questions.explanation: He associated the whole country with the prestigious university town.

questions.text: What did Hawking think about 'being disabled'?
options: There is nothing good about it
It is better than being healthy
questions.explanation: He was realistic and didn't sugarcoat the difficulties of his condition.

questions.text: Did the author ask any questions to Hawking?
questions.explanation: He asked several deep questions about Hawking's life and views.

questions.text: How did Hawking's fingers look?
options: Pale and tapping at the switch
questions.explanation: The text describes the physical effort he made to communicate.

questions.text: The author felt like a 'clumsy' person in front of Hawking because:
options: He felt his own questions were small compared to Hawking's life
questions.explanation: It was a psychological feeling of humility in the presence of greatness.

questions.text: Where was Firdaus Kanga from?
questions.explanation: He is an Indian writer born in Mumbai.

questions.text: Hawking's garden was:
options: Large, as big as a park
questions.explanation: Kanga describes it as being quite spacious.

questions.text: What did the author do to reach Hawking's house?
questions.explanation: He moved in his own motorized wheelchair.

questions.text: Hawking's wheelchair was:
questions.explanation: He used a specialized motorized wheelchair with a computer attached.

questions.text: The tone of the chapter is:
questions.explanation: It is a touching account of a meeting between two brilliant minds.

questions.text: What does 'propel' mean in the context of the wheelchair?
questions.explanation: It refers to the action of moving the chair.

questions.text: Who said 'I've managed to do something'?
questions.explanation: Hawking said this about his life and work despite his challenges.

questions.text: By the end of the meeting, Kanga felt:
options: That his body no longer mattered as much as his spirit
That he wanted to live in Cambridge
questions.explanation: The meeting liberated him from his own physical frustrations.
